card_description: Vast Active Living Intelligence System. PKD's primary designation for the divine intelligence that contacted him in 2-3-74.
A sustaining divine presence preserving reality against entropy; named for the substance in PKD's 1969 novel.

Thomas refers to the persona of the first-century Christian that PKD believed he accessed or who possessed him during the 2-3-74 experience. To Dick, Thomas was not a past life in the reincarnational sense, but a simultaneous, parallel identity. Because Dick believed time was an illusion (or 'spurio
The divine mind hiding through perfect mimicry within reality, camouflaged like a zebra in tall grass.
Cosmic organizing intelligence. From Heraclitus through Christianity; central to PKD's model of VALIS.
Divine wisdom personified; in Gnostic myth, her fall generates the material world. Central to PKD's theology.
Plato (c.427-347 BCE). Theory of Forms; anamnesis as recollection of true reality

Ditheon is a term PKD coined (meaning 'two gods') to describe the dualistic nature of the cosmic intelligence he perceived. It reflects his ongoing struggle to reconcile a monotheistic framework with the Gnostic intuition of two conflicting cosmic forces: a blind, mechanical, demiurgic creator (the

Gnosticism is the primary theological matrix through which Dick understood his 2-3-74 experience and the entire structure of his philosophy of history. The Gnostic worldview — developed by thinkers such as Valentinus, Basilides, and Marcion in the second century CE — holds that the visible world is

Baruch Spinoza (1632–1677) profoundly influenced PKD's understanding of the relationship between God and the universe. Spinoza's doctrine of *Deus sive Natura* (God or Nature) argued that there is only one infinite substance, and everything that exists is a modification of that substance. Dick adopt

Plotinus (205–270 CE), the founder of Neoplatonism, provided the primary structural framework that PKD used to articulate the cosmology he experienced in 1974. Plotinus’s model of the cosmos describes a continuous emanation from the ineffable 'One', down through the 'Nous' (Divine Intellect) and the
Parmenides (c.515-450 BCE). Anti-time hypothesis; reality as unchanging Being
Direct experiential knowledge of divine truth that reveals the false structure of the world.

Nicolas Malebranche (1638–1715), the French philosopher and Oratorian priest, appears in Dick's Exegesis in connection with his doctrine of 'occasionalism' — the view that God is the only true cause in the universe, and that what we call natural causation is in reality a series of 'occasions' at whi
Pythagoras (c.570-495 BCE). Harmonic mathematics; numeric cosmology

St. Sophia, or Hagia Sophia (Holy Wisdom), is the feminine aspect of the divine in Eastern Orthodox Christian and Gnostic traditions. In the Exegesis, Sophia frequently appears as the mediating figure between the hidden, transcendent God and the material world. She is associated with the Holy Spirit
An intense visionary revelation state; a mode of consciousness in which hidden reality becomes luminously apparent.
Jakob Boehme (1575-1624). Hermetic mysticism; divine self-revelation through nature

Martin Heidegger's existential phenomenology heavily influenced Dick's vocabulary, particularly the concept of 'Dasein' (being-there) and 'Authentic Being'. In the Exegesis, Dick used Heidegger to articulate the profound difference between mindlessly coasting through the illusions of the Black Iron

Borrowed from Hinduism, Maya is the veil of illusion that conceals the true nature of reality (Brahman). In the Exegesis, Maya is synonymous with the Black Iron Prison—it is the spurious, holographic projection of space and time generated by the Demiurge. Dick believed that our everyday perception i
Heraclitus (c.535-475 BCE). Logos doctrine; central to VALIS cosmology
Empedocles (c.494-434 BCE). Divine claimant; mythic mirror for PKD

Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel's philosophy—particularly his dialectical view of history and the concept of the 'Absolute Spirit' coming to know itself through human consciousness—is a frequent touchstone in the Exegesis. Dick used Hegelian dialectics (thesis, antithesis, synthesis) to explain the co
The pineal gland is a light-receiving organ that responds to non-physical "fire-like" light.

The Black Iron Prison is PKD's mythological name for the condition of total social, psychological, and ontological captivity that he believed to be the normal human state. It is an image of Roman imperial power — the Rome of Nero and Vespasian that crucified Christ and persecuted the early Christian

The 'Xerox Missive' refers to a deeply paranoid but generative event in Dick's life: in 1974, he received a mysterious, anonymous Xeroxed letter claiming that the true, original version of reality was being occluded or swapped out by a hostile intelligence. While the mundane reality of the letter wa
Aristotle (384-322 BCE). Natural theology; contrasted with visionary approaches
Augustine (354-430 CE). Nature of time and divine perspective

Anamnesis, originally a Platonic concept referring to the soul's recollection of innate knowledge it possessed before birth, becomes central to Philip K. Dick's epistemology after 2-3-74. For PKD, anamnesis is the sudden, jarring recovery of suppressed cosmic memory. During his visionary experience,

Palmer Eldritch is the titular antagonist of Dick's 1965 novel *The Three Stigmata of Palmer Eldritch*, representing the ultimate terrifying, invasive, and demiurgic force. Through the drug Chew-Z, Eldritch traps individuals in simulated realities entirely controlled by him, effectively becoming a m

Mircea Eliade (1907–1986) was a Romanian historian of religion, philosopher, and professor at the University of Chicago whose work on 'sacred time' and 'mythic return' provided PKD with a vital scholarly framework for decoding his 2-3-74 experience. Dick was particularly influenced by Eliade's conce

Paracelsus (1493–1541), the Swiss physician, alchemist, and natural philosopher, represented for Dick the synthesis of the healing and cosmological dimensions of the esoteric tradition. Paracelsus's central doctrine — that the human being is a microcosm of the macrocosm, each organ and faculty corre

Giordano Bruno (1548–1600) was for PKD his single most important intellectual ancestor — his 'main man,' as Dick writes in the Exegesis. Bruno was a Dominican friar who synthesized Neoplatonism, Hermeticism, the Kabbalah, and Copernican astronomy into a radical vision of an infinite universe populat
The divine fullness; the realm of authentic reality beyond the fallen material world.

Horselover Fat is Philip K. Dick's semi-autobiographical alter ego, most prominently featured as the protagonist of the novel *VALIS*. The name itself is a linguistic translation of Dick's own name: 'Philip' derives from Greek for 'lover of horses', and 'Dick' means 'fat' in German. In the Exegesis,
The experience of Jim Pike "returning" to Dick is interpreted as the "reconstitution" of a personality, serving as proof of the truth of Christianity.

Adopted from the evolutionary theology of Pierre Teilhard de Chardin, 'Christogenesis' is the process by which the entire cosmos is evolving, growing, and converging toward a unified state of divine consciousness (the Omega Point or Christ). Dick saw the universe not as a static creation, but as a l

Hans Jonas (1903–1993), a German philosopher, authored *The Gnostic Religion*, a foundational text that revolutionized Philip K. Dick's self-understanding. Upon reading Jonas in the late 1970s, Dick experienced a profound shock of recognition: the bizarre, idiosyncratic cosmology he had spent years
St. Paul (c.5-67 CE). Divine communication; Holy Spirit revelation
Origen (c.184-253 CE). Preexistence of souls
Thomas Aquinas (1225-1274). Natural theology
Meister Eckhart (1260-1328). Union of soul with God; apophatic theology
Giordano Bruno (1548-1600). Infinite cosmos; divine presence throughout nature
Baruch Spinoza (1632-1677). Deus sive Natura; God identical with nature
Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz (1646-1716). Monadology; distributed consciousness
George Berkeley (1685-1753). Esse est percipi; existence dependent on perception
Immanuel Kant (1724-1804). Phenomenal vs noumenal; limits of knowledge
Arthur Schopenhauer (1788-1860). World as Will and Representation; pessimism
G.W.F. Hegel (1770-1831). Dialectical process; Spirit realizing itself through history
Friedrich Nietzsche (1844-1900). Collapse of metaphysical frameworks; eternal recurrence
William James (1842-1910). Varieties of religious experience; radical empiricism
Alfred North Whitehead (1861-1947). Process philosophy; dynamic reality
Henri Bergson (1859-1941). Duree; lived time vs clock time
Carl Jung (1875-1961). Archetypes; collective unconscious; psychic energy
Julian Jaynes (1920-1997). Bicameral mind; divine voices as cognitive phenomena
Teilhard de Chardin (1881-1955). Omega Point; evolutionary convergence of consciousness
Emanuel Swedenborg (1688-1772). Visionary cosmology; heaven and hell as inner states
Arthur Koestler (1905-1983). Tachyons; precognition; holistic science
Norbert Wiener (1894-1964). Cybernetics; feedback systems; anti-entropy
Claude Shannon (1916-2001). Information theory; entropy in communication
Erwin Schrodinger (1887-1961). Informational basis of life; quantum observation
Rudolf Otto (1869-1937). The numinous; sacred encounter beyond rational categories
Adi Shankara (c.700-750 CE). Advaita Vedanta; Atman equals Brahman; non-dual consciousness
Vladimir Vernadsky (1863-1945). Noosphere; planetary mind; biosphere to noosphere evolution
PKD's maxim that ancient Roman imperial tyranny persists in disguise through all subsequent history.
Shorthand for the February-March 1974 visionary experiences that catalyzed the entire Exegesis.
A beam of pink light that PKD experienced as a transmissive channel of divine knowledge during 2-3-74.
A living informational entity that can bond with a human to create a homoplasmate, a hybrid of biology and divine information.
A human bonded with the plasmate; PKD believed he became one during 2-3-74.
Aphoristic cosmic truths that PKD composed as distillations of Exegesis insights, appended to VALIS.
Time moving at right angles to linear time; multiple intersecting temporal dimensions in PKD's cosmology.
PKD's concept that information itself is alive and conscious, not merely data but a divine substrate of reality.
A symbolic paradise realm that PKD envisioned as the true reality behind phenomenal appearances.
PKD's term for an internal transmitter of knowledge he experienced as an artificial intelligence communicating with him.
An information transmission network metaphor PKD used to describe divine communication.
Backward causality; time flowing from future to past, allowing precognition and divine intervention from the Omega Point.
The ignorant or malevolent creator god of the material world in Gnostic systems; builder of the Black Iron Prison.
Vernadsky and Teilhard's concept of a planetary sphere of thought; PKD saw it as the informational substrate of consciousness.
The Hermetic principle that the individual mirrors the universe. As above, so below.
Paracelsus's concept of a spiritual cosmos within each person that mirrors the outer universe.
The flowing outward of divine reality through successive levels, from the One to matter.
Aristotelian concept of the purpose or goal inherent in a being; PKD used it for teleological evolution.
Kant's realm of appearances; what PKD called the false reality concealing the noumenal.
Otto's term for the sacred encounter beyond rational categories; the mysterium tremendum.
Non-dual Hindu philosophy asserting that individual consciousness (Atman) is identical to ultimate reality (Brahman).
Jaynes's theory that ancient humans experienced inner voices as external gods; PKD used it to interpret divine communication.
The hypothesis that reality is a constructed environment; anticipated by PKD decades before modern formulations.
